title: Listening - Part 4: Returning to a Childhood Hobby
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people describing why they took up a hobby again after many years

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been a competitive swimmer as a teenager, but I dropped it when I started my career. [21]I recently went back to the pool because I was looking for a low-impact way to improve my fitness after a knee injury[/21]. It was hard at first. However, [26]the most surprising benefit has been the extraordinary sense of mental clarity I feel after a session[/26]. It’s like the repetitive laps wash away all my work stress. I now find that I’m much more creative and patient for the rest of the day, which has been a real boost for my performance at the office. || Speaker 2: I used to spend every weekend drawing, but [22]I took it up again primarily to reconnect with a part of my identity that I felt I’d lost in the corporate world[/22]. I wanted to be more than just my job title. [27]What I find most challenging is the frustration of knowing that my technical skills have significantly declined[/27]. I remember being much better at fifteen than I am now at forty. It’s been a humbling process of relearning the basics, but I’m slowly getting my 'eye' back. || Speaker 3: My grandfather taught me how to restore old clocks when I was a boy. [23]I decided to start doing it again as a way to honor his memory and feel closer to him after he passed away[/23]. It’s been very emotional. [28]The most rewarding aspect has been the discovery of a small but dedicated online community of fellow collectors[/28]. We share parts and advice, and it’s given me a whole new social circle that I never would have found otherwise. It’s made a solitary hobby feel much more communal. || Speaker 4: I’d played the piano for ten years, but I stopped when I had kids. [24]I recently started playing again because I wanted to set a good example for my daughter who is just beginning lessons[/24]. I wanted to show her that learning is a lifelong process. [29]The most significant outcome has been the improvement in my manual dexterity and coordination[/29]. I’d started to feel a bit stiff in my hands, but the daily practice has made them much more flexible. It’s a physical benefit I hadn't even considered. || Speaker 5: I was a keen gardener as a child, helping my dad in the allotment. [25]I took it up again because I was feeling increasingly anxious and needed a hobby that would force me to slow down and be present[/25]. Working with the soil is incredibly grounding. [30]What I appreciate most is the lesson in patience that the garden teaches you[/30]. You can't rush a tomato plant! This newfound ability to wait and let things happen in their own time has helped me massively in my high-pressure job, where I used to be much more reactive and impatient.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what motivated each speaker to return to their hobby.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions improving fitness after a knee injury.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to reconnect with an identity lost in the corporate world.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to honor his grandfather's memory.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to set a good example for her daughter.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 needed a hobby to help with anxiety and force her to be present.

options.A: to prove a point to a colleague
options.B: to manage their levels of anxiety
options.C: to prepare for a specific competition
options.D: to aid recovery from a physical problem
options.F: to save money on professional services
options.G: to rediscover an earlier sense of self

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of their hobby.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions an extraordinary sense of mental clarity after swimming.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions the frustration of his technical skills declining over the years.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 highlights the discovery of an online community of collectors.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 notes an improvement in manual dexterity and flexibility in her hands.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 appreciates the lesson in patience that has helped in her high-pressure job.

options.A: the irritation of being less proficient than before
options.B: a more tolerant approach to professional life
options.C: the realization that they had grown bored of it
options.D: the discovery of a hidden talent
options.F: the high cost of specialized equipment
options.G: an improvement in a physical function
options.H: the chance to share their passion with others
